steps.caption_md: Seed the queue with every rotten orange up front — just (0,0) here — and count the fresh oranges (3) that still need to be reached.
Minute 1's wave (just (0,0)) has been processed: both fresh neighbors, (0,1) and (1,0), rotted together and were enqueued. `fresh` drops to 1.

predict.prompt_md: The queue now holds the two oranges that just rotted, `[(0,1), (1,0)]`. What determines exactly when minute 2 ends and minute 3 begins?

choices.label_md: Snapshot `len(queue)` (2) before processing, pop exactly those 2 oranges as one wave, then increment `minutes` again for the next wave.
Keep popping one orange at a time, incrementing `minutes` after every single pop.
Stop immediately since only one fresh orange remains.

predict.explanation_md: `for _ in range(len(queue))` snapshots how many oranges belong to the current wave before any new ones are enqueued — the same level-size trick from tree BFS, now marking a minute boundary instead of a tree level.

steps.caption_md: Minute 2's wave rots the last fresh orange, (1,1). `fresh` reaches 0 — the loop condition `while queue and fresh` will stop after this wave finishes.
`fresh == 0`, so every orange rotted. Return `minutes = 2` — the round the last fresh orange turned, found without ever restarting BFS from scratch.
